text:
Politics and political conflict are fundamentally expressed through language. While scholars have long recognized the importance of the written and spoken word, the prohibitive costs of analyzing even moderately sized text collections have historically hindered their integration into political science research. Automated text analysis offers a solution to this challenge by drastically reducing the labor and time required to process large-scale datasets.

With increasing frequency in recent years political science has been introducing new approaches to studying politics that employ advanced computational methods on large quantities of political text data. In this piece we give a general overview of the field and, through specific examples, document some instances in which the promised potential of these approaches is being realized across several subfields of political science. We also mention a few of the pitfalls of this approach, including that computers do not replace important theoretical insights and nuanced interpretations that can come only from a close reading of text. It will also more than likely require large amounts of data, testing and fine-tuning in order to achieve meaningful results. This piece offers an overview of some popular approaches to computational content analysis, which can analyze text in countless ways (e.g. categorize sentences, assign scores to statements, discover topical structures, etc) while providing tips for evaluating the legitimacy and reliability of computational content analysis results while debunking some common criticisms that are inherent to the approach. Increasing the use of computational content analysis approaches as tools in the social sciences will also depend on the development of new computational approaches as well as methodologies to ensure the reliability and validity of their analyses.
